evals.name: react-button-accessibility
evals.prompt: I'm building a custom <CheckoutButton> in a React app and I keep getting flagged in accessibility audits. The button is icon-only (a shopping cart svg) with a number badge for the cart count. How do I make this thing actually accessible — like, screen readers reading the right thing, keyboard works, focus visible, the count gets announced when it changes? I'm using Tailwind for styles.
evals.expected_output: Concrete React + Tailwind steps for the icon-only button: ARIA labelling, live-region behavior for count changes, focus-visible styling, native <button> over div. Sources cited.

assertions.text: search-log.md exists at the outputs directory and is non-empty
search-log.md records at least 3 distinct WebSearch or WebFetch operations
search-log.md documents an evolving plan — initial sub-questions AND at least one reshape move (add/remove/pivot/confirm)
At least one load-bearing fact in answer.md was verified at a primary source via WebFetch (MDN, W3C, WAI-ARIA APG)
answer.md mentions React AND Tailwind by name and addresses all four areas (naming, keyboard, focus, count announcement)
Uses native <button> rather than div with role=button
Gives the icon-only button an accessible name (aria-label, aria-labelledby, or visually-hidden text)
Handles count-change announcement using an ARIA live region (role=status / aria-live / aria-atomic)
Marks the SVG as decorative (aria-hidden="true") when a separate accessible name is set
answer.md ends with a Sources section of markdown links

evals.name: github-actions-aws-secrets-oidc
evals.prompt: What's the actual current best practice for storing secrets in GitHub Actions workflows that deploy to AWS? I keep seeing OIDC mentioned but our older workflows are using long-lived IAM user access keys stored as repo secrets, and I'm not sure if we should migrate or how big a deal it is.
evals.expected_output: Clear OIDC recommendation, migration path, honest urgency assessment. Sources cited.

assertions.text: search-log.md exists at the outputs directory and is non-empty
search-log.md records at least 3 distinct WebSearch or WebFetch operations
search-log.md documents an evolving plan — initial sub-questions AND at least one reshape move
At least one load-bearing fact verified at a primary source (docs.aws.amazon.com, docs.github.com, or aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials repo)
Addresses the user's actual situation (currently using long-lived IAM access keys as repo secrets), not a generic OIDC explainer
Clearly recommends OIDC + role-to-assume over long-lived IAM access keys
Mentions GitHub OIDC issuer / IAM identity provider setup
Mentions aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials OR permissions: id-token: write
Provides concrete migration steps (ordered recipe), not just 'you should migrate'
Gives an explicit 'how big a deal' assessment with urgency framing
answer.md ends with a Sources section of markdown links

evals.name: homelab-reverse-proxy-choice
evals.prompt: I need to pick a reverse proxy for a small homelab — about 12 services behind it, mix of static sites and a couple of Node apps. I'm comfortable with plain nginx configs but I keep hearing Caddy and Traefik are easier for auto-HTTPS. What should I actually use?
evals.expected_output: Explicit pick tailored to homelab + 12 services + nginx-comfort + auto-HTTPS. Costs of pick named. Sources cited.

assertions.text: search-log.md exists at the outputs directory and is non-empty
search-log.md records at least 3 distinct WebSearch or WebFetch operations
search-log.md documents an evolving plan — initial sub-questions AND at least one reshape move
At least one load-bearing fact verified at a primary source (caddyserver.com, doc.traefik.io, nginx.org/nginx.com)
Anchored to user context: homelab, ~12 services, already knows nginx, auto-HTTPS — not a generic comparison
Covers all three (nginx, Caddy, Traefik) — none silently dropped
Makes an explicit pick (names ONE), not 'it depends'
Names a cost / limitation / tradeoff of the recommended option
Says when one of the non-picked options would have been the right call
answer.md ends with a Sources section of markdown links
